title: Replacing a seat pad on a wooden bar stool
subtitle: Remove the old fabric and foam, cut new foam to size, and re-staple the cover
excerpt: How to remove and replace the padded seat on a wooden bar stool. Covers taking apart the seat pad, replacing the foam insert, cutting and stapling new upholstery fabric, and reattaching the seat to the stool frame.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Upholstery. Traditional UK upholstery trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Most wooden bar stools have a removable seat pad: a circular or square plywood base board screwed to the stool frame from underneath, onto which foam is glued and fabric is stapled. The seat separates from the frame by removing four to six screws from the underside of the frame. Once off the frame, the seat pad is treated as a simple upholstery panel: foam and fabric replaceable in the same sequence as a chair drop-in seat.

content.text: unscrew from the frame, making them straightforward to replace without special tools. The seat itself is identical in construction to a dining chair drop-in seat: a plywood panel with glued foam and a stapled fabric cover. A set of four stools takes about 2 hours to re-cover.

Upholstery fabric (for a 350 mm diameter stool seat, cut a 600 mm square per seat). 40 to 50 mm high-density upholstery foam (cut to the seat pad perimeter). Upholstery contact adhesive for foam-to-board. T50 staples (8 mm). Staple gun. Staple remover. Upholstery shears. Cordless drill for removing and refitting the seat screws.
Step 1, remove the seat pad from the frame

Turn the stool upside down. Locate the screws holding the seat pad to the frame; usually three to six screws equally spaced around the perimeter of the seat mounting plate. Remove them with the cordless drill. Lift the seat pad off the frame.
Step 2, strip the old seat pad

On the underside of the seat, use the staple remover to lift all existing staples from the plywood panel. Remove the old fabric. The old foam may be glued in place; peel it off. If the plywood panel is sound (no delamination or rot), reuse it. If it has split, cut a replacement from 12 mm plywood to the same template.
Step 3, cut and glue new foam

Cut foam to exactly the shape of the plywood top; for a circular seat, use the plywood as a template. Draw around it with a marker on the foam and cut with a sharp bread knife using a sawing motion. Apply upholstery contact adhesive to both the plywood top and the foam bottom face, allow both surfaces to tack (1 to 2 minutes), then press the foam firmly onto the plywood. The bond is immediate on contact, so position carefully before pressing down.
Step 4, cut and staple new fabric

Centre the fabric face-down. Place the padded seat face-down on the fabric. Pull the fabric up and over one side and staple to the underside of the plywood. Pull the opposite side, apply even tension, and staple. For a circular seat, pleat the fabric neatly around the curve: pull the fabric to the underside at 50 mm intervals, making a small fold at each pull point to absorb the excess, and fire a staple at each pull. Work opposite sides alternately to maintain even tension. Trim the excess fabric close to the staple line. Reattach the seat pad to the stool frame.

attrs.symptom: Pleats around a circular seat create visible ridges on the top face
attrs.cause: Pleats too large or pulled from the wrong direction.
attrs.fix: The pleats must be folded under the seat (underside), not across the face. Keep each pleat fold small (10 to 15 mm at the fold) and pull more frequently — every 30 to 40 mm around the curve rather than every 50 mm.
